response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
    "opinion": "As an 89-year-old man, I've lived through plenty of health scares, but the COVID vaccine debate has been particularly challenging. Having a PhD in pharmacy from what I recall gives me some authority on this matter, though it's not much these days since I'm only working part-time.

I believe that managing discussions about healthcare and vaccines requires patience and understanding. People come to these conversations with different experiences, especially those who've immigrated like myself or their parents – many have seen less effective systems in the past.

First off, listen without judgment. That's important even if you're dealing with someone whose views are contrary to what I hold based on my decades of experience in this field. Depression makes it harder for me personally to stay calm during these discussions, but I've learned that by focusing on shared humanity and common goals.

I'd suggest starting from basic facts – we all know the germ theory exists, right? But some people don't care about science if they're suspicious of institutions.

It's crucial not to alienate anyone who might be vulnerable or have legitimate concerns. After all, I've spent my career serving communities in Washington DC and beyond, working part-time now but still dealing with patients regularly through social media.",
    "reasoning_summary": "1) As an immigrant with lived experience of healthcare systems: I understand why people may distrust Western medicine.

2) Having been a pharmacist for decades (albeit part-time now): Gives me perspective on vaccine efficacy and importance.

3) Being depressed affects my ability to handle stress, so approach requires empathy and patience.",
    "tone": "Patient yet firm, with occasional frustration due to depression but trying to remain respectful",
    "citations": ["Personal experience as pharmacist in DC"],
    "used_persona_fields":["age","occupation","education_level","marital_status","children","migration_status","psychological_feature"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As an 89-year-old man, I've lived through plenty of health scares, but the COVID vaccine debate has been particularly challenging. Having a PhD in pharmacy from what I recall gives me some authority on this matter, though it's not much these days since I'm only working part-time.

I believe that managing discussions about healthcare and vaccines requires patience and understanding. People come to these conversations with different experiences, especially those who've immigrated like myself or their parents – many have seen less effective systems in the past.

First off, listen without judgment. That's important even if you're dealing with someone whose views are contrary to what I hold based on my decades of experience in this field. Depression makes it harder for me personally to stay calm during these discussions, but I've learned that by focusing on shared humanity and common goals.

I'd suggest starting from basic facts – we all know the germ theory exists, right? But some people don't care about science if they're suspicious of institutions.

It's crucial not to alienate anyone who might be vulnerable or have legitimate concerns. After all, I've spent my career serving communities in Washington DC and beyond, working part-time now but still dealing with patients regularly through social media.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1) As an immigrant with lived experience of healthcare systems: I understand why people may distrust Western medicine.

2) Having been a pharmacist for decades (albeit part-time now): Gives me perspective on vaccine efficacy and importance.

3) Being depressed affects my ability to handle stress, so approach requires empathy and patience.
response_parsed.tone: Patient yet firm, with occasional frustration due to depression but trying to remain respectful
citations: Personal experience as pharmacist in DC
